select-alias.label: Column Aliases
select-alias.summary: How to rename columns in the output using AS.

q4.title: Write a SELECT statement using a column alias

sk1.title: What is a Column Alias?
sk1.bodyMarkdown:
A column alias in SQL lets you rename a column in your query results. This is useful for making output easier to read or for giving columns more meaningful names. You create an alias using the `AS` keyword in your `SELECT` statement.

For example:

```sql
SELECT product_name AS name
FROM products;
```

Here, the `product_name` column will appear as `name` in the output.

sk2.title: Why Use Aliases?
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
Aliases help make your query results clearer, especially when column names are long or not descriptive. They are also useful when you perform calculations or combine columns, so you can give the result a meaningful name.

For example:

```sql
SELECT price * quantity AS total_cost
FROM products;
```

Now, the result column will be labeled `total_cost` instead of an expression.

sk3.title: Syntax Tips
sk3.bodyMarkdown:
- The `AS` keyword is optional; you can also write `SELECT price total`.
- If your alias has spaces or special characters, use double quotes: `SELECT product_name AS "Product Name"`.
- Aliases only affect the output, not the actual table or data.

q1.title: What does the AS keyword do in a SELECT statement?

options.a: It renames a column in the output.
options.b: It adds a new column to the table.
options.c: It filters rows based on a condition.

q2.title: Which of the following are valid uses of column aliases?

options.a: Renaming a column for easier reading.
options.b: Changing the actual column name in the table.
options.c: Giving a name to a calculated column.
options.d: Hiding a column from the output.

q3.title: Order the steps to use a column alias in a SELECT statement.
q3.prompt: Put the steps in the correct order.

q4.title: Write a SELECT statement using a column alias
q4.prompt: In the `products_catalog` dataset, select the `price` column and give it the alias `cost`. Show only the `cost` column in the result.
